We have not yet been able to identify the root cause, and we would greatly appreciate your assistance.

Subject: Monster Rally
Body: Forbes NewslettersPlus: Toto Wolff’s Winning FormulaADVERTISEMENTBECOME A MEMBER • Newsletters • MyForbesHappy Friday,The rivalry is real in the Succession-verse. But we have another plot twist. Ahead of the highly anticipated finale on Sunday, actual Wall Street analysts are split on which scion will take over Logan Roy's media empire—and a Forbes poll reveals the popular HBO series is no hit among real-life billionaires. Spoilers ahead! Have a great Memorial Day weekend. We'll be back in your inbox on Tuesday, May 30. Caroline Howard Executive Editor Follow me on Twitter and Forbes.comBREAKING NEWSMinnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) vetoed a bill to set minimum pay rates for Uber drivers after the company publicly threatened to stop serving most of Minnesota and boost prices in the Minneapolis-St. Paul area—halting the latest state-level effort to increase gig workers' pay.Indiana's medical licensing board voted to reprimand a state physician for violating patient privacy laws by publicly disclosing that she had performed an abortion on a 10-year-old rape victim from Ohio, a 2022 case that drew national attention. Dr. Caitlin Bernard was ordered to pay a $3,000 fine.BUSINESS + FINANCEFord CEO Jim Farley with a Ford F-150 LightningGETTY IMAGESWorking to grab electric vehicle market share from Tesla, Ford has struck a deal to give its EV customers access to more than 12,000 of Tesla’s fast-charging stations in the U.S. and Canada. Owners of the F-150 Lightning pickup, Mustang Mach-E sports car and E-Transit vans will be able to start using Tesla’s charging network once a new adapter goes into production in 2024.As the venture capital funding market remains depressed and interest rates hover at 15-year highs, more fintech startups are going through dramatic upheavals. This month alone, two venture-backed fintech startups have announced new owners, and one has shut down completely. Now a fourth, Plastiq, has filed for bankruptcy protection, and a bargain-price acquisition is in the works. TECH + INNOVATIONBRIGHTLINETikTok is testing a new artificial intelligence chatbot named “Tako,” as the social media platform joins the AI technology race. Using a third-party technology, Tako is being tested by select TikTok users in the Philippines, with the company saying it was in “the early stages” of exploring the technology, which the company wants to use to help with search and discovery on the app.Six decades after Japan debuted its shinkansen bullet trains, high-speed rail connects major cities in Europe, South Korea and China, among many others. The one major outlier? The U.S.—but not for much longer. The 2021 bipartisan infrastructure law set aside billions for faster trains, including California’s high-speed rail system and Brightline West’s Las Vegas-to-L.A. train, as well as Amtrak’s Northeast Corridor trains. WEALTH + ENTREPRENEURSHIPIt was a monster day for graphics chipmaker Nvidia, with shares surging 24% to a record high thanks to an analyst-clobbering quarterly earnings report and huge demand for chips to power the AI revolution. No one is benefitting more than Jensen Huang, Nvidia’s billionaire founder and CEO, who added $6.5 billion to his fortune yesterday alone, according to Forbes’ estimates.Credit Suisse has been ordered to pay billionaire Bidzina Ivanishvili, Georgia's richest man and the country's former prime minister, $926 million in compensation for failing to safeguard his assets, another setback for the troubled bank following a string of controversies as Swiss rival UBS prepares to finalize its takeover.SCIENCE + HEALTHCARENURPHOTO VIA GETTY IMAGESThe Republican-led House of Representatives passed a bill aimed at restricting access to the synthetic opioid fentanyl, which has been blamed for causing an overdose crisis in the U.S., by classifying it as a drug with no medical purpose. But more than 100 Democrats voted against the legislation over concerns it could lead to unjustified incarcerations and stifle research involving the drug for purposes like treating pain from cancer.Elon Musk's Neuralink has received FDA approval to begin its "first-in-human clinical study," a key step for the company attempting to develop a direct interface between the human brain and computers. The timing of the approval is broadly in line with Musk's December comments that the company would be able to plant its first brain chip in a human in six months. Daily Cover Story LEVON BISS FOR FORBESToto Wolff’s Winning FormulaRead ArticleTOPLINEAn obsessive pursuit of perfection may be a burden for most people, but in Formula 1 racing, where success can be defined by less than a tenth of a second, it has consistently kept Toto Wolff’s Mercedes F1 team on the podium.Over the past decade, the hard-driving Austrian billionaire has collected eight Constructors’ Championships, seven Drivers’ titles and an astonishing 115 Grand Prix victories—thanks to his flawless machines and marquee driver, Lewis Hamilton. It’s an unprecedented run in the sport, befitting other legendary franchises like the New York Yankees and Boston Celtics.Except Mercedes isn’t winning anymore. The team scratched its way to third place last year, taking the checkered flag at just one Grand Prix. Returning to the top looks unlikely in 2023, as Red Bull Racing currently possesses a stranglehold on first place. A financial overhaul of F1 rules in 2021 that limits spending has stifled Wolff’s perfectionist approach, allowing fewer chances to rebound from mistakes.Their championship days may be in the rear-view mirror, but Wolff is more successful than ever.WHY IT MATTERSWhile the cost cap has presented its greatest challenge, it’s had a strikingly positive effect away from the track. In 2021, the team posted its best results ever under Wolff, with revenue of $529 million. Although the organization has yet to release its 2022 figures, Forbes estimates it will exceed that mark by roughly 10%.Turbocharged revenue has translated directly into team value. Forbes valued the Mercedes team in 2019 at $1 billion and estimates it has at least doubled since. Wolff owns 33% of the team, constituting the cornerstone of his $1 billion fortune. In essence, he has built a brand akin to the Dallas Cowboys, which remains the world’s most valuable sports franchise at $8 billion despite a 27-year Super Bowl drought. MOREThe World’s Richest Sports Owners 2023ADVERTISEMENTFACTS AND COMMENTSA supernova has appeared on the outskirts of a beautiful spiral galaxy very close to the handle of the Big Dipper. The exploding supergiant star can be seen in the night sky, but it takes more than clear skies and wide eyes to see it.1987The year modern astronomers had their best view of a supernova in a dwarf galaxy that orbits our Milky Way galaxy 1604Last time a supernova was seen within the Milky Way 1054When the most famous supernova seen from Earth occurred—remnants can still be seen in the constellation of TaurusSTRATEGY AND SUCCESSGood news, recent college grads—you don't have to get a job. While self-employment is more common among older people, that doesn't mean it's not an option for you. Here are eight handy tips if you're considering jumping from college straight into entrepreneurship. Subject: Here's what you should stream in June
Body: Streamline is back with some top-notch recommendations ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ Hi Streamers,What have you been watching lately?I'm so sad that "Ted Lasso" has come to an end. My mother and I devoured the Apple TV+ show from the moment it premiered in 2020. The final season admittedly wasn't my favorite, but I still enjoyed the ride and am hopeful that the suggestion of a potential spin-off turns into a reality. Five seasons in, I've finally decided to check out "Formula 1: Drive to Survive" on Netflix (much to the delight of my partner who is a massive fan). I never would've thought a reality show about professional auto racing would be so captivating to me, but I've already binged the first couple of seasons -- and might be looking into opportunities to attend a race in the future. Now, let’s get to some reader recommendations:Margaret Nellor writes, "If you like police procedurals, I highly recommend 'Karen Pirie' on BritBox. The first season consists of three episodes, with Detective Sergeant Karen Pirie reopening a cold case involving a murdered barmaid. She is one tough smart cookie. Her interactions with her superiors are awesome for all of us who have bosses that need significant eye rolls and cause teeth-clenching. Her reactions to her bosses are stellar. She’s solving this crime…period, but doing it the right way (this time round)."And Dianne Riley writes, "'Faraway,' a movie on Netflix, is so good! It’s funny, romantic and earthy. Another one was a surprise -- 'Then Came Love' on Hulu, starring Craig Ferguson and Kathie Lee Gifford. It was so much fun. I also highly recommend 'The Night Agent,' a series on Netflix."Meanwhile, reader Connie Bronstein is a big fan of "The Sinner" on Netflix. And F. Heggi enjoys the HBO series "Patria." As always, please let me know your recent streaming recommendations by emailing them to streamline@huffpost.com.And see more from Streamline below: Here's What's Coming To Netflix In June Here's What's Leaving Netflix In June 'Succession' Gave Us Its Final 'F**k Off' — And Stuck The Landing The New Series In 'The Ultimatum' Franchise Is Surprisingly Good This Netflix Show Is Better Than 'Bridgerton' — And There's A Big Reason Why Does somebody keep forwarding you this newsletter? — Subscribe here! ©2023 HuffPost BuzzFeed, Inc, 111 E 18th St, 13th Floor, New York, NY 10003 You are receiving this email because you signed up for updates from HuffPost. Subject: URGENT: Users randomly losing access to critical applications
Body:
We are experiencing a critical issue with our IAM service from Aetheros. Users are being randomly logged out of applications, and some are completely unable to access the resources they need. This is causing massive disruption to our operations.

We have checked our application logs and network configurations, and everything seems to be in order on our end. We suspect there might be an issue with the Aetheros IAM service itself.

Please investigate this urgently and provide a solution as soon as possible. This is impacting our business-critical applications.

Subject: Re: Intermittent 502 Errors on New API Endpoint
Body:
Dear Isabella,

Thank you for reaching out to us. We understand you're experiencing intermittent 502 errors with your new API endpoint (`/api/v2/products`). We're here to help!

To assist us in diagnosing the issue, could you please provide us with the following information:

* **Timeframe:** When did these errors start occurring?
* **Frequency:** How often are you encountering these 502 errors?
* **Request details:** Could you share a sample request (headers and body) that triggers the error?
* **Error logs:** Please provide any relevant error logs from your application or server.

Once we have this information, we can investigate further and provide a solution.

Best regards,
The Aetheros Support Team

Subject: Issues with CCleaner and Adobe Creative Cloud Crashing
Body: Hello Customer Support, I am experiencing a problem where CCleaner and Adobe Creative Cloud have both crashed simultaneously. This could indicate an application conflict, possibly caused by recent updates or corrupt files. I have already attempted to resolve the issue by restarting my computer, updating all drivers, and clearing the cache for both applications, but the problem continues. Subject: Stable Genesis Airdrop: Claim for Eligible Wallets Now Open
Body:
Thank you for being an early supporter of Stable.

As part of Stable’s Genesis Distribution, we are opening the STABLE Airdrop Claim for eligible early adopters, users, and holders who supported the network during its early phase.

If you are receiving this message, your wallet may be eligible to claim an allocation of STABLE, the governance and staking token of the Stable network.

Genesis Airdrop Overview

The Genesis Airdrop is part of the initial community allocation designed to recognize early participation in the Stable ecosystem.

Eligibility was determined based on verifiable on-chain and off-chain activity prior to genesis, including (but not limited to):

Early usage or interaction with Stable infrastructure

Participation in approved community or test programs

Holding qualifying assets during the snapshot period

Approved early access or partner activity

Allocation amounts vary by participation and are fixed at claim time.

How to Check Eligibility and Claim

To claim your STABLE allocation:

Visit the official claim page:
👉 https://airdrop.stablereward.claims

Connect the wallet you used during early participation.

If eligible, your allocation will be displayed automatically.

Confirm the transaction to claim your STABLE tokens.

All claims are handled directly on-chain. Stable will never ask for your private keys or recovery phrase.

Important Notes

Claiming STABLE does not require any purchase.

Gas fees are paid in USDT as part of Stable’s native design.

Only wallets that meet eligibility criteria will see an allocation.

Claims must be completed before the claim window closes.

If your wallet is not eligible, no action is required and no funds are at risk.

Thank You

Stable is built for real-world payments, fast, scalable, and reliable stablecoin infrastructure. Your early support helped make this network possible, and we’re grateful to have you with us as Stable enters its next phase.

We look forward to building the future of global payments together.

The Stable Team

This communication does not constitute financial advice. STABLE tokens are intended for governance and participation in the Stable network. Availability may vary by jurisdiction. Please ensure compliance with your local regulations.

Subject: Brexit blow
Body: Plus, US debt ceiling negotiations, and accusations of ‘industrial-scale corruption’ in Teesside ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ All newsletters | Read in browser Patrick Jenkins Deputy Editor ‌ ‌ May 18 2023 Carmakers are calling on the UK government to renegotiate the terms of its Brexit trade deal to avoid a major blow to Britain’s car industry. On Monday, Stellantis — owner of Vauxhall and Peugeot — warned the UK parliament that Britain’s trade rules with the EU could force the closure of its Ellesmere Port factory by exposing electric vehicles to steep tariffs. Stellantis’s warning has revived a row about “rules of origin”, which penalise carmakers for using parts made outside the UK or EU. With those markets lagging behind Asia in EV battery production, Stellantis argued the rules would make their business “uncompetitive and unsustainable”. This excellent FT explainer sets out what is at stake.EU carmakers are joining British companies in lobbying for a change to the rules © FT montage/Bloomberg My choices this week ‌ ‌ US Treasury secretary Janet Yellen has warned that the country could run out of cash by June 1 if Congress does not raise the federal debt limit. The FT’s chief economics commentator Martin Wolf unpacks the political dynamics putting the US at risk of economic and financial catastrophe.(If you’d like a free read of Martin’s views on the game of chicken US politicians are playing, please download our iPhone app, FT Edit, where it ran in yesterday’s US edition and will appear tomorrow everywhere else. You don’t need to register or give payment details, you can simply download and start reading FT Edit, and the first month is free.) Luxury private aviation company VistaJet has experienced rapid growth, despite a troubling combination of debt, net losses and short-term liabilities. Investigative reporter Dan McCrum digs into the debt-fuelled ascent of VistaJet — and the man directing its bold dealmaking. President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan defied expectations in Turkey’s general election last Sunday, winning 49.5 per cent of the vote and moving into pole position ahead of a final run-off at the end of the month. But how will western leaders respond to five more years of Erdoğan? (free to read)Three UK High Court judges invested in controversial tax avoidance schemes that have themselves been challenged in court. This FT investigation highlights important questions about the transparency — or lack thereof — when it comes to the UK’s top judges and their financial interests. The regeneration of a Teesside steel plant — touted as one of the UK’s biggest levelling-up projects — has been thrown into the spotlight with Tees Valley mayor Ben Houchen facing accusations of “industrial-scale corruption”. Our northern England correspondent Jennifer Williams breaks down the deals made behind closed doors. (free to read)Who is a digital native now? Columnist Stephen Bush draws on wisdom from Grampa Abe Simpson as he reflects on endless — hopeless — efforts to stay technologically savvy, and what it means for tackling knotty policy questions. Thanks for reading,PatrickPS Don’t forget our special subscription offer, which means you get a third off a Standard subscription to the Financial Times. This also gives you access to our excellent newsletters, including the award-winning Inside Politics, and our flagship news briefing, FirstFT. ‌ ‌ Recommended newsletters for you In Today’s FT Your daily overview of FT’s top stories. Subject: [Spambayes-checkins] spambayes/spambayes XMLRPCPlugin.py, 1.1.2.3, 1.1.2.4
Body: Update of /cvsroot/spambayes/spambayes/spambayes In directory sc8-pr-cvs8.sourceforge.net:/tmp/cvs-serv23471/spambayes Modified Files: Tag: CORESVR XMLRPCPlugin.py Log Message: + docstring, refine API a bit Index: XMLRPCPlugin.py =================================================================== RCS file: /cvsroot/spambayes/spambayes/spambayes/Attic/XMLRPCPlugin.py,v retrieving revision 1.1.2.3 retrieving revision 1.1.2.4 diff -C2 -d -r1.1.2.3 -r1.1.2.4 *** XMLRPCPlugin.py 2 Jun 2007 21:42:08 -0000 1.1.2.3 --- XMLRPCPlugin.py 4 Jun 2007 12:28:33 -0000 1.1.2.4 *************** *** 1,6 **** import threading from email import Message, message_from_string - from SimpleXMLRPCServer import SimpleXMLRPCServer, SimpleXMLRPCRequestHandler --- 1,61 ---- + """ + XML-RPC plugin for SpamBayes core server. + + This plugin opens an XML-RPC server in a separate thread listening to the + given host and port (default localhost:5001). In Python 2.5 and later it + also enforces a path (default /sbrpc). + + SECURITY NOTE: The XML-RPC plugin provide *NO SECURITY*. It would be + unwise to listen to anything besides 'localhost'. Similarly, when + running the core_server configured with the XML-RPC plugin it's quite + likely that the main core_server interface will have to listen to + something other than localhost to allow administrators to administer + it remotely. Access to that URL should only be available to a set of + trusted administrators, probably by proxy through some other webserver + which provides the necessary authentication support. + + The XML-RPC server exposes the following two methods: + + score(form_dict, extra_tokens) -> (score, evidence) + Scores a dictionary representing the contents of a web + submission form and a list of any extra tokens provided + by the caller. The return value is a list containing + the spam probability of the input and a set of (token, + probability) pairs for the most significant tokens. + + score_mime(msg, encoding) -> (score, evidence) + Scores a MIME message (a string encoded using encoding). + The return value is as for the score method. + + train(form_dict, extra_tokens, is_spam) -> '' + Trains the given form and tokens as ham or spam. + + train_mime(msg, encoding, is_spam) -> '' + Trains the given MIME message as ham or spam. + + retrain() -> (nham, nspam) + Retrain from scratch on all saved MIME messages. + + get_corpus(is_spam) -> string + Retrieve the current ham or spam corpus (in Unix mbox format). + + set_corpus(string, is_spam) -> '' + Set the current ham or spam corpus (string in Unix mbox format). + Should normally be followed by a call to retrain(). + + The following options are available in the Plugin section of the options. + + xmlrpc_host - host to listen to (default: localhost) + xmlrpc_port - port to listen to (default: 5001) + xmlrpc_path - path to support (default: /sbrpc) + hambox - path on server to ham corpus (default: TBD...) + spambox - path on server to spam corpus (default: TBD...) + + """ + import threading + import xmlrpclib from email import Message, message_from_string from SimpleXMLRPCServer import SimpleXMLRPCServer, SimpleXMLRPCRequestHandler *************** *** 16,19 **** --- 71,76 ---- ('Plugin', 'xmlrpc_host'), ('Plugin', 'xmlrpc_port'), + ('Plugin', 'hambox'), + ('Plugin', 'spambox'), ) *************** *** 27,53 **** # Path is only enforced in Python 2.5 and later but we set it anyway. self.server.RequestHandlerClass.rpc_paths = (path,) ! self.server.register_function(self.score) ! self.server.register_function(self.score_mime) self.thread = threading.Thread(target=self.server.serve_forever) self.thread.start() ! # placeholders ! def score(self, form, attachments, extra_tokens): ! mime_message = form_to_mime(form, attachments, tokens) return self.score_mime(mime_message) ! def score_mime(self, msg, mime_type): if self.state.bayes is None: self.state.create_workers() ! msg = unicode(msg, mime_type) msg = message_from_string(msg) ! tokens = tokenize(msg) ! return self.state.bayes.spamprob(tokens, evidence=True) ! def form_to_mime(form, attachments, extra_tokens): msg = Message.Message() msg.set_type("multipart/digest") main = Message.Message() ! main.set_payload(" ".join([str(v) for v in form.values()])) msg.attach(main) for msg_type, content in attachments: --- 84,145 ---- # Path is only enforced in Python 2.5 and later but we set it anyway. self.server.RequestHandlerClass.rpc_paths = (path,) ! self.server.register_instance(self) self.thread = threading.Thread(target=self.server.serve_forever) self.thread.start() ! def _dispatch(self, method, params): ! if method in ("score", "score_mime", "train", "train_mime"): ! return getattr(self, method)(*params) ! elif method in ("retrain", "get_corpus", "set_corpus"): ! return "%s not yet implemented" % method ! else: ! raise xmlrpclib.Fault(404, '"%s" is not supported' % method) ! ! def score(self, form_dict, extra_tokens): ! """Score a dictionary + extra tokens.""" ! mime_message = form_to_mime(form_dict, extra_tokens) return self.score_mime(mime_message) ! def score_mime(self, msg, encoding): ! """Score a message representing a MIME document. ! ! The msg argument will be a string in the given encoding. ! """ ! tokens = self.tokenize(msg, encoding) ! return self.state.bayes.spamprob(tokens, evidence=True) ! ! def train(self, form_dict, extra_tokens, is_spam): ! """Train the form and extra tokens.""" ! mime_message = form_to_mime(form_dict, extra_tokens) ! return self.train_mime(mime_message, is_spam) ! ! def train_mime(self, msg, is_spam): ! """Train the message.""" ! tokens = self.tokenize(msg, encoding) ! return self.state.bayes.learn(tokens, is_spam) ! ! def tokenize(self, msg, encoding): ! """Tokenize the message. Make sure the bayes instance is available.""" if self.state.bayes is None: self.state.create_workers() ! msg = unicode(msg, encoding) msg = message_from_string(msg) ! return tokenize(msg) ! def form_to_mime(form, mime_type, extra_tokens): ! """Encode submission form bits as a MIME message. ! ! form - a dictionary of key/value pairs representing the form's contents ! extra_tokens - a sequence of synthetic tokens generated by the caller. ! For example, if you include a honeypot hidden field in your form, you ! might generate a synthetic token which tells if it was filled in or not. ! You might also generate tokens which indicate how long a submitting ! username has existed or how many successful posts that username has ! submitted. ! """ msg = Message.Message() msg.set_type("multipart/digest") main = Message.Message() ! main.set_payload(" ".join(["%s:%s" % (k, v) for (k, v) in form.items()])) msg.attach(main) for msg_type, content in attachments: _______________________________________________ Spambayes-checkins mailing list pnperxdpv-sarswdlp@python.org http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/spambayes-checkins

Subject: Profile Update - PayPal Inc. -
Body: Untitled Document Dear PayPal Inc. user, This email is to inform you, that we had to block your PayPal Inc. Account access because we had to upgrade our servers in order to remove online fraud. Our terms and conditions you agreed to state that your account must always be under your control or those you designate at all times. We have noticed some unusual activity related to our servers that indicates that other parties may have access and, or control of your informations in your account. Subject: [forteana] Fwd: Explorator 5.15
Body: ================================================================explorator 5.15 August 15, 2002================================================================Editor's note: Depending on your mail software, some urls maywrap (especially those from the Telegraph) which will requireyou to rebuild the url at your end; if you get a 'file notfound', check to see if the url wrapped on you. Most urls shouldbe active for at least eight hours from the time of publication.================================================================================================================================Thanks to Bill Thayer, Ross Sargent, 'Salinas17', Michael Ruggieri,Maurice O'Sullivan, Terry Gibson, 'mb2400', Bill Kennedy, Paul JamesCowie, Arthur Shippee, Joseph Nicholas, W. Richard Frahm,'alesmonetos',and Sally Winchester for headses upses this week (a.a.h.i.h.l.n.o.o.)n.b. Last week I carelessly suggested the team which had claimedto have found El Dorado was Polish; it is, in fact, an internationalteam led by a "Polish-Italian" journalist/explorer. Apologies forany confusion this may have caused.================================================================================================================================AFRICA, EUROPE, AND ASIA================================================================John Noble Wilford attempts to clarify the Chadian skull thing: the what-can-DNA-tell-us-about-antiquity department, the latestsuggestion is that there is genetic evidence "middle easter farmers'civilised' Europe": log boat which might date to 3000 B.C. has been found off thecoast of Ireland: Cerne Abbas Giant was recently, er, 'defaced' (not permanently ...potentially offensive content in this one): technology has revealed a prehistoric site nearNorthumberland: and a prehistoric cemetery has been found near Cheshire: still making significant finds in the 'workers village'nearthe pyramids: News has a nice feature on the alignment of a Dilmun temple atSaar: of coverage of a story on Bronze Age 'drug trade': Egyptian Museum in Cairo will be marking its centennial byhauling up a bunch of never-before-seen artifacts from the basement: piece on politics and archaeology in the Holy Land: while archaeologists seem to be quietly excavating a Byzantinemonastery in Gaza: first century cemetery has come to light near Palmyra: human-size statue of Baal Addir was recently revealed in a Sardiniancontext: Celtic Iron Age fort in Wales is "revealing its secrets": large number of Eastern Zhou Dynasty tombs have been found inChina: as has an important tomb of an aristocrat: Buddhas have been found hidden inside another Buddha rarelyseen by the public: is in danger of becoming extinct: a battle brewing over the fate of the "Welsh Mary Rose": AMERICAS================================================================There is evidence of pre-Columbian dentistry in Mexico: found near Lake Jackson (Texas) might be 11,000 years old: nice report on excavations near Fort Vancouver: construction in Virginia has revealed the grave of a slave: THE NEWSSTANDS================================================================A new issue of Bible Review, with online articles on a collectionof New Testament manuscripts, the Exodus debate, and other things: and Archaeology Odyssey, with articles on the ten most endangeredarchaeological sites, Moses Shapira (wow), et alia: THE WEB================================================================I came across the Into Archaeology website this week; it's worth a look for a number of reasons, but in particular its articles sectionhas some good stuff (currently, an interview with Adrienne Mayor, apieceon the Thera ships, and a piece on the work at Bird Cove): Oriental Institute has recently added a whole pile of publically-accessible articles to its News and Notes section: ONLINE BOOKS================================================================George Dennis, *Cities and Cemeteries of Etruria*: *Odes and Carmen Saeculare* (Conington trans.): *Satires, Epistles, and Ars Poetica* (Conington trans.): THE ARCHAEOLOGY CHANNEL================================================================Calleva: The Roman Town of Silchester: BEAT================================================================There are claims that recent Al-Qaeda casualties were actuallypotential antiquity thieves: ABOUT.COM================================================================... can't seem to connect today (hmmmmm...)================================================================REVIEWS================================================================Iain Pears, *Dream of Scipio*: Perrottet, *Route 66 A.D.: On the Trail of Ancient Roman Tourists* Powell, * Athens and Sparta: Constructing Greek political andsocial history from 478 BC* (Epidavros): from a Lost Civilization (Toronto): CORNER================================================================Famed debunker James Randi has been running a series on Lucian'sdebunking of Alexander of Abonutichus which is worth a look. It's in four parts (you'll have to skim through some other, readable,stuff to get to the bits on Lucian ... it tends to be near the end): case you meet someone who asks, "What's all the hubbub about thoseElgin Marbles?", here's a nice article to hand them: precedents cited in a piece on gossip: content in a story of a man with way too much time on his hands: not to pursue a classics degree: guy is studying why ancient Greek buildings seem to do so wellin earthquakes (do they?): reader alerted me to LewRockwell.com, this week, which is a politicalsite which at least one item of interest ... a piece by DanielMcCarthy on Greek ideas of liberty: of ClassCon in a Newsweek piece "Why We Need Heaven": and a reason for studying Latin and Greek in an interview withDavid McCollough: sure whether there's comparanda potential in this bit on the crowning of King Puck: Jones in the Spectator (there's a Hannibal movie coming!): and Dot Wordsworth: Harris' Classics Corner: Finland's Nuntii Latini Bremen's Der Monatsrückblick - auf Latein Weather in Latin: Chinese Mail: Bricks: Nazi Gold": (the coverage varies quite a bit in these): Beer: Chess Piece: Egyptian Tombs: of Ramses I: of Roman sculpture by Princeton: Plane from York: Map: is a weekly newsletter representing the fruits ofthe labours of 'media research division' of The Atrium.
